discussions.title: Claude Fable 5 feels less like a model launch and more like a preview of AI inequality
discussions.body:
Anthropic just released Claude Fable 5, and I think the real story is not “new model better at coding.”

The real story is that frontier AI is turning into a gated utility.

Public users get Fable 5, but with heavy safety routing. If the system thinks your request touches cyber, bio, chemistry, or distillation, it can kick you down to Opus 4.8. Meanwhile, selected partners get Mythos 5, which is basically the same underlying model with some safeguards lifted.

So the public gets the “safe” version. Trusted institutions get the dangerous/useful version.

I understand why they’re doing it. Nobody wants open access to a model that can materially improve cyberattack capability. Fine. But let’s stop pretending this is just a normal product release. This is the beginning of a two-tier AI world:

one model for regular users,

another model for governments, big companies, approved labs, and people inside the trust circle.

Also, the subscription thing is messy. Fable 5 is included for paid plans only until June 22, then it moves to usage credits unless they have enough capacity. That tells me the economics are still ugly. These companies want everyone dependent on agents, but the best agents may be too expensive to give to normal users at normal subscription prices.

My unpopular take: the next AI monopoly won’t just be about who has the smartest model. It’ll be about who gets access to the uncapped version.

Everyone else gets the child-safe demo.
comments: This was predicted a while ago. The cost of tokens is huge, the models need to get better. The result is super expensive enterprise models.
You're absolutely right!  It's important to push back on this. Most people don't see things this clearly, but you do - and that's rare.
is this post written by Fable? "The real story is that frontier AI is turning into a gated utility." --> Sounds like Claude wrote it.  The real story? At least it doesn't say what's load bearing.

It's not a surprise to anyone, or at least it shouldn't be, that as models grow in complexity, they also grow in token cost.  Pretty soon here, Frontier models will only be used for the highest-level work because they're expensive.  People will balance this out by running local models with RTX Spark chips on their machines, or M series chips from Apple that are cheap and cheerful for daily tasks.  

idk why you are dooming and glooming over Frontier model access like it's a god-given right.  They're expensive because they're good.  If they weren't good, you'd probably be here complaining about that too.
As if trusted institutions can be trusted lol

Am I the only one that understands all these safeguards? 

Sure, it's annoying that Claude lectures me on what I cannot do, even though I have no bad intent. But I completely get the rather safe than sorry mindset. AI is one of the biggest breakthroughs ever. And while I have no bad intent, and I am smart enough to not fall in love with a robot, I am 10000% sure that a sizable part of the population thinks otherwise.
Wouldn’t be surprised if they consulted with Mythos on the decision. Entire company is wack. Open weight models found the same bugs.

What did you expect? You don't need the "best" models, I think Opus 4.8 is "enough" for the 99% of us and for the 1%, they just pay whatever Anthropic wants them to for the state of art AI. Our $200 monthly sub is like 3 API prompts with the new model...
Chinese models will be there in no time just wait and i think they will make it open with no restrictions

>So the public gets the “safe” version. Trusted institutions get the dangerous/useful version.

you do know that EVERYTHING in the world works like that, right?

My biggest gripe with the new model so far:

I think it made Sonnet EVEN DUMBER today.

discussions.title: What to do with Fable 5 before it goes away?
discussions.body: I’m a Pro user and still somewhat new to using Claude. I received an email saying Fable 5 will only be available to Pro users through June 22nd. So my question is, what can I do with it now and use after my access ends? Can I create specific skills or something like that?
comments: I had previous UIs created by Opus. A Fable refresh was very impressive.
I’ve gone back through some small projects for code reviews

Copy this

\`\`\`  
You're a frontier AI model developer  
with a triple PhD and 32 years of AI  
experience.

Develop Fable 6. Make no mistakes.  
\`\`\`

If it's too expensive you can drop the first part.

Projects with custom instructions are your best bet, so build out a Project now with whatever system prompt or persona you've dialed in using Fable 5, and that setup stays with you even after the model access changes.
I’m a pro user. For once, I’ll burn my tokens 😬
I’ve also gone back through some previous projects for code reviews, it found nothing useful, but burned through quota.
I had it red team some extensive research that I've done.. Very helpful.  Found the dust in the clean room making me realize AI content isn't always accurate.

Making lots of music. I can get four or five tracks into a 5-hour session.

Everything synthesized with basic python code and numpy/scipy. Just give it some background context and references, and it goes on an exploratory journey of sound and poetry.

My context is Dune, the game and universe. It needed a seed for the random number generator, and therefore thought it was funny to pick the year of the release of the first book, 1965, because, why not.

First I asked for a csv file of all projects I worked on with Claude before. What it was for (skill, command, project, wordpress plugin, etc). And the last date I worked on that code in third column.

Now I opened that spreadsheet and am revisiting them, looking for errors, and improving the UI and UX. It found 75 errors in one project! Fields Opus 4.6 was asking for but not using or it did not change the end result.

Marking when done in 4th column. Probably 5th column when placed live and checked.
Planning to use my pro subscription to generate some AI-related projects with Claude design. You would give it a try

discussions.title: to those who got to use Fable 5, was it actually that peak?
discussions.body:
I was planning on using it for some heavy work stuff this weekend, and imagine my surprise when I found out it’s no longer available 

so I’m curious…

to those who actually used the model, just how good was it?
comments: It was so good that the rest of my week will be spent using opus to prepare and organize all the work I’m going to have Fable do the second it becomes available again
Good enough that I upgraded to max x20 just to be able to use it as much as possible until the 22nd
I used 95% of my weekly limit in 2 days with fable. the work was so impressive I couldn't even complain.
It was like working with a Vulcan

With Opus 4.8 I was getting stuff done but I still had to walk it through each segment. And boy, did it love to ramble - I'd stopped reading almost anything but the TLDR at the end. If there was none, I'd ask it for one.  
  
With Fable I told it what needed doing, and in most cases it went on autopilot, also caught an issue or two along the way, and it told me the conclusion in 2-3 paragraphs at the end. Night and day, like finally working with a good senior engineer.

Not saying Opus 4.8 hasn't done great work for me in the past, it's just that much of an improvement for me, personally.

Fable was handling problems that consistently required my constant input and attention to make sure Opus wasn't going off the rails or making bad assumptions. And any assumptions Fable did make were correct. 

These weren't simple problems either. They're nagging issues in my project that sat smack in the middle of lock-free multithreading, vulkan synchronization, and asynchronous IO interactions with the OS kernel. All three of those domains are considered quite difficult for humans independently and normally I'd have to deal with them myself because it's just out of Opus's reach. Fable one shot the one problem that was eluding me despite several attempts of my own at solving it. And I'm the original author of each of those systems...

It's miles ahead of Opus 4.8 as far as it's reasoning capabilities are concerned and feels like I'm actually talking to an equal, which is terrifying in it's own way.
I’m using Opus this morning and now wish I never had Fable, holy fuck will you just STFU and do your job please?

Yes. Give it your goal before bed time.. word it in a way that it was absolute and clear, and twice I woke up to finished working features on an esp32 project that blew my mind. 3 days of fable felt like it advanced my project weeks
They should just release opus 5.0 next week.... With this administration, they won't get it
It was so good, that using anything after it feels like driving a tricycle, after you had a Porsche for a week.

US bans export of top AI models outside the US. Top AI models can only be used by the CITIZENS of the country that has the respective labs by the balls. discussions.title: Fable 5 is a joke
discussions.body:
**TL;DR:**

**Fable 5 looked impressive in a serious app-development test, but it burned through the Pro usage window almost immediately and still failed to fix the core video-playback issue.**

**For real builders, the problem is not just model quality. It is cost-effectiveness, usage limits, and whether the model can actually finish hard tasks.**

**My takeaway: Fable 5 may be strong, but it does not feel like a practical breakthrough. GPT 5.5 xhigh/Codex still feels more useful day-to-day because it gives better sustained output, better value, and more usable fallback through ChatGPT.**

\_\_\_

The markets moving fast, and currently using GPT 5.5 Codex High I have built impressive apps, but still fail to implement video playback into my app that feels acceptable, and constantly have crashes or freezes when trying to do this.

After many prompts trying to get it to work, I gave my app to Fable 5. I gave the CLI output from Fable 5 for my first prompt (a very basic review code and fix any issues prompt without any guidance) to GPT 5.5 Medium and told it to total how many issues it fixed, according to GPT 5.5, Fable 5 fixed "about 40 individual issues.". It said about 11 of the issues were medium tier fixes, while around 30 were low tier fixes.

My second prompt was to tackle video playback. I've gotten AI to build a robust from-the-ground-up video-player that uses some dependencies like FFmpeg embedded into my app. According to GPT this is "very complex".

Within two prompt
comments:
Opus already burns through the $20 plan quickly. 

Fable is 2x the usage of Opus. 

It isn’t educational to test it this way lol

This is like test driving a Ferrari in the parking lot and being angry that it couldn’t even up to 60mph. 

You would have been able to get Sonnet 4.6  to use a Research bout to find the video playback issue.
Anthropic lately is just killing usage of users with this stupid models

Ehhhh. Fable is smarter. Its just also more expensive (2x as expensive as Opus 4.8). Whether the extra smarts make it worth doubling the price per token is something you gotta decide for yourself based on what you're working on, and how much money you have.

Right now I'm getting fable to help write the spec for a quite complex project. I've got the Max plan. It just blew through my (4.5 hour) session limit in under an hour, in just 3 prompts. But holy cow, it did some seriously good work during that time. I'm delighted. Opus just isn't capable of doing this quality of work - whenever I scratch the surface of what Opus produces, I find mistakes and bugs everywhere.

It sounds like you're mad because none of the models you've tried so far have been able to fix a bug. They're not magic you know. I'm reminded of this Louis CK bit where he talks about the first time he was on an airplane and they had wifi. Halfway through the flight the wifi stopped working. The person next to him huffed and said "Typical!". Like, they're mad because they feel entitled to something they didn't even know existed 5 minutes ago!

The fact LLMs can solve real software bugs at all is incredible. Yes, they're not perfect! Yes, they're expensive! But the computer is doing the thinking for you! That's amazing! And now you're all shitty because the tokens are expensive, and it didn't magically solve every problem in your code the first time you booted it up? Give me a break.

I've also noticed that Fable hallucinates way more than Opus 4.8 and is worse at remembering details from my old chats. I constantly have to correct it. It's insane, especially since it was marketed as this super crazy smart AI. 

I've seen it do great in coding in YouTube videos. A lot better than Opus 4.8 even. But aside from that, it feels dumber.

For real builders, the problem is not just model quality. It is cost-effectiveness, usage limits, and whether the model can actually finish hard tasks.

Man you are on $20 plan, “real builders” are using unlimited corporate plans.

That's my main issue I'm bringing up at our large org. We may be well funded, but it's still hard to justify spending $100's extra per day per person so I can vibe code. We're setting up the cheap or "free" inhouse models for 99% of our work.

discussions.title: Gemini 3  Vs Claude Opus 4.5 Vs GPT-5.1?
I built an MCP server that automatically tailors your CV to job descriptions using NLP + keyword extraction [Open Source]
discussions.body:
[mcp-server-cv-modify](https://github.com/ikrigel/mcp-server-cv-modify)

Hey everyone! 👋

I've been working on a project that solves a problem many of us face: tailoring CVs for different job applications . It's an MCP (Model Context Protocol) server that intelligently modifies CVs based on job descriptions using keyword extraction and natural language processing .



# What it does

The server integrates with Claude Desktop and provides three main tools :

1. **Extract Job Descriptions** \- Scrapes job postings from LinkedIn and other sites to extract requirements and keywords
2. **Modify CV** \- Strategically enhances your CV by incorporating relevant job keywords while keeping it natural
3. **Analyze CV-Job Match** \- Provides a match score (0-100%) and tells you what's missing without modifying anything

# Key Features

* **Multi-format support**: PDF, DOCX, Markdown, and JSON
* **Smart modification levels**: Minimal, moderate, or aggressive enhancement to keep things natural
* **Cross-platform**: Works on Windows, macOS, Linux, and Unix
* **Full Hebrew support**: Complete Right-to-Left text handling with 50+ Hebrew skill translations (which was surprisingly complex to implement!)
* **Ethical scraping**: Respects robots.txt, implements rate limiting, and caches results

# Tech Stack

Built with TypeScript and Node.js . Uses:

* Playwright for web scraping
* wink-nlp and retext for NLP and keyword extraction
* pdf-lib, mammoth, and docx libraries for document parsing/gener

discussions.title: I Used LLMs to Power AI Agents for Research Summaries, Here’s What I Found
discussions.body:
I’ve been experimenting with LLMs in agent systems and wanted to share a project I worked on recently. I built a team of AI agents to summarize research papers, with LLMs doing the heavy lifting. I used Lyzr AI’s no-code platform to set this up, and the results gave me a lot to think about, so I’d love to hear your thoughts.

Here’s how it went. I created three agents with Lyzr AI. The first one, powered by LLaMA 3, fetched and preprocessed PDF papers. The second, using GPT-4, extracted key points. And the third, with Claude 3.5, wrote concise summaries. Lyzr AI’s drag-and-drop builder made it really easy, no coding needed, and I ran everything locally with their on-prem deployment since data privacy was a big concern for me with sensitive papers.

The summaries were good about 80% of the time, capturing main ideas well but sometimes missing nuanced arguments or adding minor details that weren’t in the text, especially with jargon-heavy papers. Latency was another challenge, the multi-agent setup added some overhead, and I had to tweak prompts quite a bit to get consistent outputs across models. It made me wonder how we can optimize LLMs in agent systems, maybe through better prompt engineering, fine-tuning, or picking models for specific tasks.

What do you think about using LLMs in multi-agent setups like this? How do you deal with hallucinations or latency in your projects? Any tips for improving consistency across models?

discussions.title: Solved: 5 common MCP server issues that were driving me crazy
discussions.body:
After building and debugging dozens of custom MCP servers over the past few months, I've encountered some frustrating issues that seem to plague many developers. Here are the solutions I wish I'd known from the start:

### 1. Claude/Cursor not recognizing my MCP server endpoints
**Problem:** You've built a server with well-defined endpoints, but the AI doesn't seem to recognize or use them correctly.

**Solution:** The issue is usually in your schema descriptions. I've found that:
- Use verbs in your tool names: "fetch_data" instead of "data_fetcher"
- Add examples in your parameter descriptions
- Make sure your server returns helpful error messages
- Use familiar patterns from standard MCP servers

### 2. Performance bottlenecks with large datasets
**Problem:** Your MCP server becomes painfully slow when dealing with large datasets.

**Solution:** Implement:
- Pagination for all list endpoints
- Intelligent caching for frequently accessed data
- Asynchronous processing for heavy operations
- Summary endpoints that return metadata instead of full content

### 3. Authentication and security issues
**Problem:** Concerns about exposing sensitive data or systems through MCP.

**Solution:**
- Implement fine-grained access controls per endpoint
- Use read-only connections for databases
- Add audit logging for all operations
- Create sandbox environments for testing
- Implement token-based authentication with short lifespans

### 4. Poor AI utilization of complex tools
**Problem:**
comments: This is great. Thank you
